findings.title: McpServerConfig allows invalid transport/field combinations
findings.code_snippet:
export interface McpServerConfig {
  transport: TransportType
  command?: string
  args?: string[]
  url?: string
  headers?: Record<string, string>
}
findings.description: Encapsulation: 4/10, Expression: 3/10, Usefulness: 6/10, Enforcement: 5/10 (Aggregate: 4.5). McpServerConfig and McpServerConfigInput use flat optional fields for transport-specific properties. When transport is 'stdio', command is required but typed as optional; when transport is 'sse' or 'http', url is required but typed as optional. The type permits illegal states such as { transport: 'stdio', url: 'http://example.com' } with no command, or { transport: 'http', command: '/bin/node' } with no url. Runtime validation in validateInput partially compensates but only for the add() path -- the type system itself provides no protection, and update() bypasses validation entirely.
findings.suggestion: Refactor into a discriminated union keyed on transport: type StdioServerConfig = { transport: 'stdio'; command: string; args?: string[]; url?: never }; type SseServerConfig = { transport: 'sse'; url: string; headers?: Record<string, string>; command?: never }; type HttpServerConfig = { transport: 'http'; url: string; headers?: Record<string, string>; command?: never }; type McpServerConfig = (StdioServerConfig | SseServerConfig | HttpServerConfig) & { id: string; name: string; enabled: boolean; createdAt: number; updatedAt: number }. Apply the same pattern to McpServerConfigInput.

findings.title: update() skips all input validation, can persist invalid state
findings.description: Encapsulation: 5/10, Expression: 3/10, Usefulness: 5/10, Enforcement: 2/10 (Aggregate: 3.75). The update() method applies a partial merge via spread and filterUndefined() without re-validating the resulting config. A caller can change transport to 'stdio' without providing command, or change transport to 'http' while leaving url undefined. The add() path calls validateInput() but update() has no equivalent check. This can corrupt the database with configs that fail at connection time.
findings.suggestion: After merging partial onto existing, call validateInput() (or a new validateConfig() that checks the full merged object) before persisting. Better yet, with discriminated union types (see review-types-001), the type system would catch most of these at compile time.

findings.title: No Valibot schema validation at Imperative Shell boundary
findings.description: Encapsulation: 5/10, Expression: 5/10, Usefulness: 5/10, Enforcement: 4/10 (Aggregate: 4.75). McpServerStore is an Imperative Shell boundary that accepts external input (McpServerConfigInput) and persists it to SQLite. The project standard is Valibot for boundary validation, but this module uses manual if-checks in validateInput(). Manual validation is incomplete (does not validate name length, url format, transport enum membership) and fragile (easy to forget a check when fields are added). This is a recurring pattern in this codebase -- previously flagged for DocumentStore.
findings.suggestion: Define a Valibot schema for McpServerConfigInput (e.g., v.variant('transport', [stdioSchema, sseSchema, httpSchema])) and call v.parse() at the store boundary. This also synergizes with the discriminated union recommendation in review-types-001.

findings.title: McpTransport union lacks common discriminant property
findings.description: Encapsulation: 5/10, Expression: 4/10, Usefulness: 5/10, Enforcement: 5/10 (Aggregate: 4.75). The McpTransport union mixes a class instance (Experimental_StdioMCPTransport, which has no 'type' property) with two plain object variants that do have a 'type' discriminant. This makes the union non-discriminated -- consumers cannot use a simple type guard like 'if (transport.type === "stdio")' to narrow the type. The resulting ergonomic penalty is visible at mcp-client-manager.ts:20 where an unsafe 'as McpTransport' cast is needed.
findings.suggestion: Wrap all three variants with a consistent discriminant: type McpTransport = { type: 'stdio'; transport: Experimental_StdioMCPTransport } | { type: 'sse'; url: string; headers?: Record<string, string> } | { type: 'http'; url: string; headers?: Record<string, string> }. This enables clean narrowing and eliminates the cast.

findings.title: Unsafe 'as' cast on DB row transport field bypasses type safety
findings.description: Encapsulation: 5/10, Expression: 3/10, Usefulness: 5/10, Enforcement: 3/10 (Aggregate: 4.0). McpServerRow.transport is typed as plain 'string' (matching SQLite's TEXT column), and rowToConfig() casts it to TransportType with 'as'. If the DB contains a value not in the TransportType union (e.g., from a migration or manual DB edit), the cast silently produces an invalid McpServerConfig. Similarly, row.args and row.headers are JSON.parse'd without validation.
findings.suggestion: Add a runtime guard before casting: function isTransportType(s: string): s is TransportType { return ['stdio', 'sse', 'http'].includes(s) }. Throw an error if the DB value is not a valid transport. For args/headers, use Valibot v.parse(v.array(v.string()), JSON.parse(row.args)) to validate parsed JSON.

findings.title: aggregateTools/getTools return Record<string, unknown> losing all type info
findings.description: Encapsulation: 6/10, Expression: 2/10, Usefulness: 3/10, Enforcement: 5/10 (Aggregate: 4.0). Both getTools() and aggregateTools() return Promise<Record<string, unknown>>, which erases all type information about MCP tool definitions. Consumers receive an untyped bag of unknowns, requiring unsafe casts or runtime checks to use any tool. The @ai-sdk/mcp library likely provides a typed return from client.tools() -- the explicit Record<string, unknown> annotation may be unnecessarily weakening the type.
findings.suggestion: Use the return type from @ai-sdk/mcp's client.tools() directly (e.g., ReturnType<McpClient['tools']>) or define a McpToolSet type alias that preserves the SDK's type information. If the SDK type is genuinely Record<string, unknown>, consider wrapping it in a branded type or interface to at least document the expected shape.

findings.title: Timestamp fields use bare 'number' (recurring primitive obsession)
findings.description: Encapsulation: 5/10, Expression: 4/10, Usefulness: 4/10, Enforcement: 5/10 (Aggregate: 4.5). createdAt and updatedAt are typed as bare 'number', a recurring pattern in this codebase (previously flagged for context-engine, persona-engine, and orchestrator). A branded type like 'EpochMs' would express intent, prevent accidental arithmetic with seconds-epoch values, and make the codebase more self-documenting. Severity lowered to P3 because this is a codebase-wide pattern that should be addressed holistically rather than per-module.
findings.suggestion: Define a branded type: type EpochMs = number & { readonly __brand: 'EpochMs' }. Apply across the codebase in a dedicated follow-up. For now, add a JSDoc annotation: /** Unix epoch milliseconds */.

positive_observations: TransportType is a proper 3-value string literal union, not a plain string -- good domain modeling
McpServerStore encapsulates the SQLite database as a private field with clean constructor initialization (WAL mode, table creation in a try/catch that closes on failure)
McpClientManager properly encapsulates the clients Map as private and exposes only typed methods -- no leaking of internal state
ServerStatus is a 2-value literal union rather than a boolean, enabling future states like 'connecting' or 'error'
McpServerStore.validateInput() checks transport-specific field requirements at runtime, even though this should be expressed at the type level
McpHub composes store and client manager with proper lifecycle management (shutdown closes both)
Separation of McpServerConfig (persisted) from McpServerConfigInput (creation) follows command/query separation
